claimed_contribution: The paper proposes a conceptual framework for local brand-name creation in multilingual international markets that combines linguistic translation analysis and cognitive analysis.
It distinguishes three translation methods for phonographic-to-logographic name creation - phonetic, semantic, and phonosemantic - and links them to practical branding decisions.
It shows experimentally that translation effectiveness is contingent on contextual factors, especially which script is emphasized and what translation approach is standard in the category.
context: The study is set in the Chinese market, where many brands use dual writing: an original English name in alphabetic script and a Chinese local name in logographic characters. Respondents are Chinese consumers with basic English knowledge, representing an important young target segment for multinational brands.
design_and_data: The authors first developed fictitious English brand names and Chinese phonetic, semantic, and phonosemantic translations, then pretested familiarity and likelihood of use across six product categories. Experiment 1 (n = 183, Shanghai college students; 3 x 2 between-subjects) manipulated naming method and language emphasis (English vs Chinese) in dual-name displays. Experiment 2 (n = 120; 2 x 2 x 2 mixed) added prior category naming method (phonetic vs phonosemantic) and included recall and recognition measures to assess memory-based alternatives. Experiment 3 (n = 240; 2 x 2 x 2) manipulated domestic versus foreign image source to test whether country-image effects explain the naming results.
main_findings: No single translation method was universally best; evaluations depended on contextual conditions.

Phonetic translations were evaluated more positively when the English name was emphasized than when the Chinese name was emphasized, while phonosemantic translations were similar across emphasis conditions in Experiment 1 and Experiment 3.

Prior category naming method produced a three-way interaction: prior phonetic naming favored phonetic names under English emphasis, whereas prior phonosemantic naming favored phonosemantic names under Chinese emphasis; these effects were not explained by foreign-image interactions.
one_line_summary: The paper develops and tests a framework showing that in English-Chinese dual-name branding, the effectiveness of phonetic, semantic, and phonosemantic translations depends on language emphasis and prior category naming conventions.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior brand-naming research that mainly focused on English name characteristics by addressing translation choices in multilingual, dual-script markets. It introduces a priming-based cognitive explanation for when different translation methods are preferred.
research_gap: Prior naming research had focused largely on English names and offered limited generalizable guidance for translating brand names across writing systems. The authors argue that no prior framework had modeled local brand-name creation in multilingual international markets where consumers process two scripts.
research_question: How should firms create local brand names when an original English name is presented together with a Chinese translation in a multilingual market? Specifically, the paper asks which translation approach - phonetic, semantic, or phonosemantic - is evaluated most favorably, and how that depends on language emphasis and prior translation norms within the product category.
